title: Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim: The Adventure Game - the biggest "but" in gaming (review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z8MJYHsQh5c

Having unboxed it earlier, Daniel (@dungeondive) delivers a full review of Skyrim: The Adventure Game — a game with, as he puts it, an enormous "but." He structures the review to mirror his experience: the bad first. The price is brutal ($149 base, $85 expansions), the rulebook is among the worst he's encountered (no glossary, no index, multiple false starts), the art is poor (screenshots of low quality, near-invisible monster chits, an ugly low-detail map), and the design leans on players already knowing the lore. He nearly sold it.

Then the big but: he loves it. It resets fast for its size and fits his small table; the character progression, loot and crafting/enchanting systems interlock beautifully with skills; combat is quick, deadly and genuinely fun (armor types, last-blow tokens, stamina as a resource). Best of all is the threat/timer system — the finest he's seen, because it lets *players* choose where threat lands and rewards engagement with more time — plus a free-roam mode that delivers the open-world questing he actually wants. A deeply flawed, overpriced, hard-to-learn game he woke up early to play. He'll compare it with Fallout and Arkham Horror 3e next.

----

Daniel singles out Skyrim's threat/timer system as the best he's seen because it rewards engaging with the game rather than rushing. What game, flaws and all, won you over enough that you kept playing anyway?
